--- Page 1 ---
Tokenization with Split Trees
Craig W. Schmidt*, †
Michael Krumdick*
Adam Wiemerslage*
Seth Ebner*
Varshini Reddy*
Yuval Pinter§
Chris Tanner*,‡
*Kensho Technologies
§Ben-Gurion University
‡MIT
Cambridge, MA
Beer Sheva, Israel
Cambridge, MA
Abstract
We introduce Tokenization with Split Trees
(ToaST), a subword tokenization method that
directly optimizes compression under a new
recursive inference procedure. ToaST greed-
ily splits each pretoken into a full binary tree
using precomputed byte n-gram counts, inde-
pendent of any vocabulary. Given a vocabulary,
inference recursively descends each split tree
and emits the first in-vocabulary node reached
on each path. Vocabulary selection is formu-
lated as an Integer Program (IP) that minimizes
the total token count over all split trees under
this inference procedure. The Linear Program-
ming (LP) relaxation is near-integral in prac-
tice, yielding provably near-optimal vocabu-
laries, with training time empirically scaling
quadratically in the number of split trees. On
English text, ToaST reduces token counts by
more than 11% compared to BPE, WordPiece,
and UnigramLM at vocabulary sizes of 40,960
and above, reducing the number of inference
tokens for models using this tokenizer, thus ex-
tending the effective context length. ToaST also
uses common single-byte tokens less frequently
than these baselines, leading to a substantial
improvement in Rényi efficiency. In experi-
ments training 1.5B parameter language mod-
els, ToaST achieves the highest CORE score,
outperforming baselines by 2.6%–7.6%, with
significance for two of three, and scoring best
on 13 of 22 individual tasks.
1
Introduction
Tokenization is an important first step in many nat-
ural language processing tasks. The most com-
mon approach, Byte Pair Encoding (BPE; Sennrich
et al., 2016), was initially designed as a compres-
sion technique (Gage, 1994), minimizing the num-
ber of tokens in a greedy, heuristic manner. Us-
ing fewer tokens has the immediate practical ben-
efits of lower inference cost and a larger effective
†Corresponding author: craig.schmidt@kensho.com
 
K
e
n
t
u
c
k
y
 
K
e
n
t
u
c
k
y
 
K
e
n
t
 
K
e
n
t
e
n
u
c
k
y
u
c
k
u
c
Figure 1: Example split tree for ␣Kentucky.
context window. The relationship between com-
pression and downstream task performance is less
clear. Some studies (Gallé, 2019; Rust et al., 2021;
Goldman et al., 2024) find a correlation, while oth-
ers (Schmidt et al., 2024; Ali et al., 2024) argue
compression alone does not explain tokenization
quality. However, these practical benefits are rea-
son enough to optimize compression directly rather
than rely on BPE’s greedy approximation.
Prior work has shown that finding a tokenizer
that maximizes compression is NP-complete (Whit-
tington et al., 2025; Lim et al., 2025), even over
bounded alphabets (Kastreva et al., 2025). These
results apply to both the bottom-up merge-based
formulation of BPE and the direct vocabulary se-
lection problem using optimal shortest-path infer-
ence (Schmidt et al., 2024). Lim et al. (2025) also
show tokenization is NP-hard under shortest-path
inference, proposing a partition cover approach in
which a greedy polynomial-time approximation al-
gorithm gives a 3% compression gain over BPE.
These hardness results suggest a need for alterna-
tive approaches.
We introduce a new subword tokenization ap-
1
arXiv:2605.22705v1  [cs.CL]  21 May 2026


--- Page 2 ---
proach that follows neither the bottom-up ap-
proach of BPE nor the alternative ablative top-
down approach of UnigramLM (Kudo, 2018) or
SaGe (Yehezkel and Pinter, 2023). Tokenization
with Split Trees (ToaST) instead uses a large dic-
tionary of byte n-gram counts to greedily split a
given pretoken into a full binary tree. The nodes in
this split tree form the candidate tokens. The split
trees are constructed solely from this precomputed
n-gram count data, without first specifying a tok-
enizer vocabulary. Given a vocabulary, inference
follows a recursive descent of the split tree. If a
given node of the tree is in the vocabulary, it be-
comes a token. Otherwise, the procedure is called
recursively on the two children in the split tree.
Tokenizer training selects the subset of the can-
didate tokens, equal to the desired vocabulary size,
that minimizes the number of training tokens, by
solving an optimization problem. We formulate
the training problem as an Integer Program (IP)
that can be relaxed and solved as a Linear Pro-
gram (LP), minimizing the total token count to
within a negligible fraction (10−6) of the global
optimum. We show computational results up to
700,000 split trees, covering 99% of the aggregated
pretoken counts of the training data, with computa-
tional time scaling quadratically with the number
of split trees. We compare ToaST to several stan-
dard tokenization baselines, and show it improves
compression by at least 11% for vocabulary sizes of
40,960 or larger, and Rényi efficiency by a compa-
rable amount. Nanochat language models of depth
24 with 1.5B parameters show ToaST achieving the
highest CORE metric, significantly outperforming
two of three baselines.
2
Split Trees
As is common practice, we first split our training
data into pretokens using a regular expression, and
then aggregate the unique pretokens by count. Let
p index the byte n-grams sp computed within pre-
token boundaries across the training corpus whose
count cp meets or exceeds a threshold cmin. For a
reasonably large value of cmin, these n-grams can
be computed efficiently (Schmidt et al., 2026, Ap-
pendix B). We can use the counts to split a pretoken
in a deterministic way: starting with the full preto-
ken, we split it into two nonempty parts with the bi-
nary split [sp, sp′] maximizing min(cp, cp′),1 with
1If no pair of splits appears in the count dictionary, then
the longest prefix contained in the dictionary is split from the
split
cp
cp′
min(cp, cp′)
␣Kentucky
986M
10,558
10,558
␣K entucky
1.37M
10,647
10,647
␣Ke ntucky
221,234
10,647
10,647
␣Ken tucky
60,162
10,699
10,699
␣Kent ucky
15,944
31,457
15,944
␣Kentu cky
9,904
70,919
9,904
␣Kentuc ky
9,900
257,430
9,900
␣Kentuck y
9,899
67M
9,899
Table 1: Example of split counts, with the selected
maximum split score shown in bold.
ties broken by choosing the leftmost split. This for-
mula encourages high counts on all nodes, avoiding
lopsided splits.2
Table 1 shows example counts for the pretoken
␣Kentucky. In this example, the fifth split with a
score of 15,944 is chosen. This procedure contin-
ues recursively until we have constructed a split
tree, with single bytes as leaves, such as the exam-
ple shown in Figure 1.
3
Split Tree Inference
Tokenization inference is performed by recursively
walking the split tree. For ease of exposition, as-
sume we have constructed a vocabulary V , such as
the one described in Section 4. At each node, if a
node is in V it is emitted as a token, and otherwise
its split point is computed and both children are pro-
cessed recursively. To avoid unknown tokens, we
assume that all 243 single bytes appearing in valid
UTF-8 are included in V .3 An implementation of
the inference algorithm for a dictionary of n-gram
counts and a vocabulary is given in Listing 1.4
Figure 2 gives an example tokenization using
split tree inference. Assume the two nodes shown
in white are not in the vocabulary, while the nodes
in blue are. Inference will first visit ␣Kentucky.
Since that is not in the vocabulary, it will recur-
sively visit the two children generated by the cho-
sen split. ␣Kent is in the vocabulary, so it will be
part of the tokenization. Since ucky is not in the vo-
cabulary, it will also be split into uck and y, which
remainder of the pretoken. See the most_known routine of
Listing 1.
2Any other method independent of the eventual trained
vocabulary could be used instead. One could, for example, find
morphologically correct splits from a gold standard reference
dataset (cf. Hofmann et al., 2021), or enforce that splits not
break a multi-byte character when possible. See Section A.
3See Appendix C of Land and Bartolo (2024) for an expla-
nation of why all 256 bytes are not present in valid UTF-8.
4Split tree inference speed is competitive with compiled
baselines; see Section B.4.
2


--- Page 3 ---
# if best_split fails,
# find the longest run we know about in counts
def most_known(s: bytes):
for i in range(1, len(s)):
if s[:i] not in counts:
return i-1 # last one in counts
def best_split(s: bytes):
best_score = -1
best_i = -1
# find the split with the maximum min value
for i in range(1, len(s)):
left_s = counts.get(s[:i], -10)
right_s = counts.get(s[i:], -10)
score = min(left_s,right_s)
if score > best_score:
best_score = score
best_i = i
# if no split has both parts in count
# fall back to this, to split it
if best_i == -1:
best_i = most_known(s)
return best_i
def tokenize(s: bytes, vocab: set[bytes]):
if len(s) == 0:
return []
# all leaves are fine
# if s in vocabulary, return it
if len(s) == 1 or s in vocab:
return [s]
# recursively call with best split
best_i = best_split(s)
return tokenize(s[:best_i], vocab) + \
tokenize(s[best_i:], vocab)
Listing 1: Split tree inference algorithm with a given
vocabulary vocab and count dictionary counts.
will be the other two tokens in the tokenization.
The nodes below these on the tree are never visited.
This inference procedure has several desirable
properties. Changing the vocabulary V has no ef-
fect on the split trees, since they are created in-
dependently from V . Any possible vocabulary V
(containing all 243 necessary single bytes) results
in a valid tokenization, unlike BPE which must also
have a set of merge rules. Finally, removing a token
t from the vocabulary V simply splits any current
tokens t into parts, with no other changes. Unlike
UnigramLM, PathPiece (Schmidt et al., 2024), or
greedy-left-to-right inference (Uzan et al., 2024),
there are no cascading effects on the tokenization.
Removal of a token t that appears in a given split
tree simply continues the recursive process of find-
ing in-vocabulary tokens further down the tree.
4
Tokenization Training
We develop an Integer Programming (IP) formula-
tion that will find the vocabulary of a specified size
m = |V | minimizing the total number of tokens
used when a training corpus is tokenized with the
split tree inference procedure. Experimentally, the

u
c
Figure 2: Example tokenization, with white tokens not
in the vocabulary, used tokens (in the vocabulary) in
blue, and nodes never reached in the recursive descent
in light gray.
Token sets
T
potential tokens in the vocabulary
V ⊂T
selected vocabulary
Index sets with corresponding element names
i ∈I
potential tokens ti ∈T
i ∈IB ⊂I
single byte tokens that must be in V
j ∈J
split trees, or pretokens
k ∈Kj
nodes in split tree j
k ∈Lj ⊆Kj
leaf nodes of split tree j
k′ ∈Ajk ⊂Kj
ancestors of node k in split tree j
Parameters
cj
aggregated count of split tree j
m
desired vocabulary size |V |
Variables
xi ∈{0, 1}
is token i included in vocabulary V
zjk ∈{0, 1}
is node k in the tokenization of split tree
j, using split tree inference
Table 2: Summary of notation.
LP relaxation of this IP can be rounded to an integer
solution that is extremely close to the guaranteed
global optimum (see Section 5). Thus, we avoid
the poor scaling often associated with solving IPs
through branch-and-bound.
4.1
Integer Programming Model
For convenience, the notation used in the model
is summarized in Table 2. The token sets consist
of sets of byte strings representing actual tokens,
while index sets are integers. The index used for
elements of each index set is also shown.
Let j ∈J be an index over the aggregated pre-
tokens in the training data, each forming a split
tree, with aggregate count cj. Let k ∈Kj be an
3


--- Page 4 ---
index over the tokens forming nodes in split tree j.
Let tjk be the token string for node k in split tree
j. Note that there may be duplicate token strings
within a given split tree j, but the index k treats
them as separate nodes. Let B be the set of 243
single byte tokens that can appear in UTF-8, which
should always be in the vocabulary to avoid out of
vocabulary tokens. Let T be the set of unique token
strings over all nodes of all split trees, combined
with B:
T =

[
j∈J,k∈Kj
tjk
 [
B.
(1)
Note that T is the set of potential tokens in the
vocabulary V , so V ⊆T. Let i ∈I be an index
over the unique tokens ti ∈T. To map token tjk to
the correct index i we define:
ijk = i ∈I|ti = tjk,
∀j ∈J, k ∈Kj.
(2)
In other words, ijk is a lookup for the correct token
index i for node k in split tree j.
Let xi be a binary variable that indicates if token
i is included in the vocabulary V ,
xi ∈{0, 1}
∀i ∈I.
(3)
Let zjk be a binary variable that indicates that node
k is part of the tokenization of split tree j after
inference is run,
zjk ∈{0, 1}
∀j ∈J, k ∈Kj.
(4)
The objective f we seek to minimize is:
f = min
X
j∈J
cj
X
k∈Kj
zjk,
(5)
which is the total number of tokens required to
tokenize the training data.
This objective is subject to the constraint that the
total number of tokens in the vocabulary V ⊆T is
exactly the desired size m = |V |:
X
i∈I
xi = m
(6)
Let i ∈IB ⊆I be the indices of the single byte
tokens B. We set xi = 1 for all single byte tokens
B, to enforce that they are in the vocabulary:
xi = 1
∀i ∈IB.
(7)
Consider the example in Figure 3. If the xijk
corresponding to the blue node en is in the vocabu-
lary, then the node will appear in the tokenization
 
K
e
n
t
u
c
k
y

e

u
c

u
c
Figure 3: A node (blue) appears in the tokenization
when it is in the vocabulary and none of its ancestors
(red) are in the vocabulary. An example path from leaf
node e to the root is also shown with a heavier black
outline.
with zjk = 1, unless one of its ancestors, shown
in red, also appears in the vocabulary. In that case,
the ancestor would block en and appear in the tok-
enization instead. More generally, zjk = 1 if and
only if xijk = 1 and xijk′ = 0 for all ancestors k′.
We need the following two types of constraints
to enforce that the zjk correctly represent the tok-
enization of each split tree j as resulting from the
inference procedure (Section 3) with the vocabu-
lary specified by the xi.
4.1.1
Ensuring Complete Coverage
First, we need to ensure that all the bytes of the
pretoken are included in the tokenization. Consider
the path of nodes from the leaf node e to the base of
the tree shown by heavier black outlines in Figure 3.
Exactly one of the nodes on the path must appear
in the tokenization. There cannot be more than one,
since the inclusion of an ancestor in the tokeniza-
tion prevents its descendants from appearing in the
tokenization. If none of the nodes appeared in the
tokenization, then the e in the pretoken would not
be included in any token of the tokenization. Thus,
we need to ensure that zjk = 1 for exactly one
node along each path from a leaf node to the root.
Let k ∈Lj ⊆Kj be an index over the leaf
nodes Lj in split tree j. Let k′ ∈Ajk be an index
over the (possibly empty) set of ancestor nodes k′
above node k in split tree j. To ensure the entire
pretoken is included in the tokenization, we need
each leaf node k ∈Lj or one of its ancestor nodes
4


--- Page 5 ---
to appear in the tokenization:
zjk +
X
k′∈Ajk
zjk′ = 1,
∀j ∈J, k ∈Lj.
(8)
Note that Eq (8) ensures that the en of Figure 3
cannot be in the tokenization if one of its ancestors
is, since exactly one of the binary zjk on the path
can be 1, forcing all others to be 0.
4.1.2
Relating the x and z Variables
All that remains is to ensure that for all nodes where
zjk = 1, we must have the corresponding xijk = 1
in the vocabulary. Conversely, having xijk = 0
implies zjk = 0, meaning we cannot use a token
that is not included in the vocabulary. Thus we
need:
zjk ≤xijk
∀j ∈J, k ∈Kj.
(9)
4.2
Final Model
In total, if we minimize Eq (5) subject to Eqs (3),
(4) and (6) to (9), we will get the vocabulary exactly
minimizing the total number of tokens in a corpus
when using ToaST inference.
4.3
LP Rounding Heuristic
Integer programming is NP-hard (Karp, 1972;
Garey and Johnson, 1979). However, the relaxed
problem, which replaces the integrality constraints
in Eq (3) and Eq (4) with bounds and allows frac-
tional values:
0 ≤xi ≤1
∀i ∈I,
(10)
0 ≤zjk ≤1
∀j ∈J, k ∈Kj,
(11)
can be solved efficiently as a Linear Program (LP).
With this model, the resulting LP solution has a
very small number of fractional values,5 which we
round with the following simple heuristic. Let x∗
i
and z∗
jk be the optimal LP solution with objective
value f∗
LP . Partition I into sets of i indices with
an xi near 1, near 0, or taking a fractional value in
between:6
I1 = {i ∈I : x∗
i ≥1 −ε},
(12)
I0 = {i ∈I : x∗
i ≤ε},
(13)
IF = I \ (I1 ∪I0).
(14)
5See Section 5 for our computational results.
6LP solutions are computed using floating point values, so
most variables are not exactly equal to 0 or 1. We use a small
tolerance ε = 1 × 10−5 when comparing to integer values.
Since there are already |I1| variables at 1, we would
like to round m −|I1| of the x∗
i for i ∈IF up to 1
to obtain the desired vocabulary size m, and round
the rest to 0. The objective contribution C∗
i of the
z∗
jk associated with each fractional x∗
i is,
C∗
i =
X
j∈J, k∈Kj: ijk=i
cj z∗
jk,
∀i ∈IF .
(15)
We use the simple rounding heuristic of rounding
m−|I1| of the x∗
i for i ∈IF with the highest C∗
i up
to 1, and the rest to 0. Rounding these x∗
i up allows
their associated nodes to be in the tokenization,
saving at least cj per node in the objective versus
rounding them down, in which case each of these
nodes would have to be represented by at least two
tokens rather than one. Given a vocabulary of all
integer xi, the corresponding integer zjk can be
found through the split tree inference procedure,
finding the first token in the vocabulary along each
path from the root, and setting that zjk = 1.
5
Scaling Results
5.1
Training Data
We train tokenizers on a portion of the English sub-
set of the multilingual dataset CulturaX (Nguyen
et al., 2024). We use a very large 175GB tokenizer
training corpus and 37.5GB of validation data for
computing intrinsic metrics. This corpus size was
chosen to be in the range of diminishing returns
found in Reddy et al. (2026).
We first pre-tokenize the training data using a
length-limited variant of the regular expression of
GPT-4o,7 which limits match length to avoid very
large split trees resulting from long runs of whites-
pace, and then compute the frequency of each pre-
token in the training corpus. The ToaST model is
trained on the most frequent pretokens. Due to the
Zipfian distribution of words (Zipf, 1949), the most
frequent 700,000 of the 58,469,075 unique preto-
kens accounted for 99.01% of the total counts of all
pretokens, which we deemed sufficient coverage
for training.8
We then compute all byte n-grams contained
within the aggregated pretokens that occur at least
cmin times, for use in creating the split trees. We use
a cutoff cmin = 250, which was set to be slightly be-
low the frequency of 272 achieved by the 700,000th
7See Listing 2 in Section B.3 for details.
8The fraction of total counts covered by other pretoken
counts is given in Table 5 in Section B.1.
5


--- Page 6 ---
most frequent pretoken, which produced 5,991,872
unique n-grams.9
5.2
Implementation
The LP models were implemented using the Py-
omo modeling language (Bynum et al., 2021; Hart
et al., 2011)10 and solved using the dual sim-
plex algorithm from the HiGHS open source LP
solver (Huangfu and Hall, 2018).11,12 For compar-
ison, baseline models for BPE, WordPiece (Wu
et al., 2016; Schuster and Nakajima, 2012), and
UnigramLM were built using the Hugging Face
tokenizers library,13 using the same length-limited
GPT-4o regex used by ToaST.
5.3
Computational Performance
The number of variables and constraints in our LP
scales linearly in the total byte-length of all unique
pretokens P
j |Lj|.14 Since pretoken lengths are
bounded and small in practice, model size is also
linear in the number of pretokens. Figure 4 shows
that, empirically, the total time to build and solve
the LP is quadratic in the number of split trees over
the range we study. This modest scaling allows us
to solve reasonably large models in practice that
cover 99% of the aggregated counts of pretokens.15
Empirically, we find that the LP solutions have
very few fractional variables, and that the objec-
tive of the rounded integer solution is extremely
close to the LP bound. We consider 400k, 500k,
600k, or 700k split trees, and 32 vocabulary sizes
ranging from 8,192 to 262,144. Across these 128
problem instances, the LP relaxation is nearly in-
tegral: in the worst case, only 66 xi variables and
285 zjk variables are fractional, corresponding to
fewer than 0.012% and 0.004% of the variables in
those instances, respectively. Let f∗
LP denote the
optimal LP objective value, and let ˆf denote the
9Thus, the n-gram dictionary covers all 700,000 pretokens,
each of which is a candidate root token in the vocabulary.
Storing these n-grams and their counts requires approximately
708MB as a Python dict[bytes, int].
10https://www.pyomo.org/
11https://highs.dev/
12Our code, including both training and split tree inference
will be open sourced following review.
13https://github.com/huggingface/tokenizers
14See Section B.2 for details.
15The runs of Figure 4 were run independently on an AWS
r7i.48xlarge instance, but note that it is possible to solve the
same model over a range of vocabulary sizes in significantly
less time using warm starts. See Section B.5 for details. This
warm-start approach was used for the vocabulary range used
in later figures.
100000
200000
300000
400000
500000
600000
700000
Number of Split Trees
0
4
8
12
16
20
24
28
32
36
40
44
Total Time (hours)
y = 9.2861e-11x² - 4.5965e-06x
R² = 0.9971
Figure 4: Total training time as a function of the number
of split trees. In these runs the vocabulary size m is set
to half the number of split trees.
objective value after rounding to an integer solu-
tion according to Section 4.3. Across the problem
instances, the maximum absolute gap ˆf −f∗
LP is
21,500 out of 37 billion, and the maximum rela-
tive gap ( ˆf −f∗
LP)/ ˆf is 5.76 × 10−7. In 44 of
the 128 cases, the LP solution is fully integral.16
Overall, the LP relaxation is exceptionally tight.
We conjecture this arises from the tree structure
itself: on each root-to-leaf path, selecting a higher
node always produces fewer tokens, giving the LP
little incentive to assign fractional values across
competing nodes on the same path.
6
Intrinsic Results
As seen in Figure 5, ToaST achieves substantial im-
provements in compression as measured by bytes
per token. ToaST achieves a 7.5% improvement
over the best baseline at a vocabulary size of 8,192,
10.5% at 24,576, and more than 11% for vocabulary
sizes of 40,960 and larger. The ToaST results are
very similar when varying the number of split trees
from 400k to 700k, except at higher vocabulary
sizes.17 Compression does improve very slightly
with more split trees, which suggests one should
use as many as is computationally feasible.
As shown in Table 3, tokens produced on the
validation data can be divided into four categories.
Root tokens encode a full pretoken, often as a se-
mantically meaningful word, which is the ideal
outcome. Unavoidable Leaves are single-byte pre-
tokens (e.g., punctuation) with only one possible
tokenization. Non-Leaf Subwords represent parts
of less frequent words. Leaf tokens are single-byte
16See Table 7 to Table 10 in Section E for the complete data
on fractional variables and relaxation gaps.
17See Figure 12 in Section C for a zoomed-in chart for
vocabularies of 196k or more.
6


--- Page 7 ---

32k
65k
98k
131k
163k
196k
229k
262k
Vocabulary Size
3.4
3.6
3.8
4.0
4.2
4.4
4.6
4.8
5.0
Bytes per Token
ToaST 400k trees
ToaST 500k trees
ToaST 600k trees
ToaST 700k trees
Theoretical limit (5.01)
BPE
Unigram
WordPiece
Figure 5: Validation data bytes per token for ToaST and
several baselines, as a function of vocabulary size. With-
out the use of supermerges (Liu et al., 2025; Schmidt
et al., 2025, 2026) there is an upper bound in which each
pretoken is a single token, which is shown as a dotted
line at 5.01 for the validation data.
0k
32k
65k
98k
131k
163k
196k

Vocabulary Size
0
2
4
6

10

Weighted Tokens (billions)
Unavoidable Leaf
Root
Leaf
Non-Leaf Subword
Figure 6: Stacked validation token categories for ToaST
with 700k split trees by Leaf (single byte tokens), Un-
avoidable Leaf (single byte pretokens), Non-Leaf Sub-
word, and Root nodes (single token for pretoken).
fallbacks within multi-token pretokens; they ap-
pear in too many contexts for the language model
to learn a meaningful representation and are best
avoided. We apply the same categorization to base-
line methods by analogy.

Bytes in tok

inference on the validation data.

egories for ToaST,18 while Figure 7 gives the same
results for BPE.19 ToaST has vastly fewer Leaf

ToaST 400k trees

ToaST 600k trees
ToaST 700k trees
BPE
Unigram
WordPiece
Figure 8: Validation Rényi efficiency (α = 2.5) for
ToaST and several baselines as a function of vocabulary
size. Higher is better, indicating a more uniform token
distribution. The four ToaST series are very similar to
each other, as are BPE and WordPiece.
tokens than all baselines with a ~14–19x reduc-
tion at a vocabulary size of 65,536. ToaST also
produces substantially more Root tokens than the
baselines. These extra Root tokens are possible
because ToaST avoids the intermediate tokens used
in BPE to reach high-frequency tokens, freeing up
vocabulary space for more Roots.
Rényi efficiency measures how uniformly a tok-
enizer uses its vocabulary; with α = 2.5 (Zouhar
et al., 2023), it particularly penalizes very frequent
tokens. Zouhar et al. (2023) suggests Rényi effi-
ciency correlates with downstream performance,
although Cognetta et al. (2024) and Dagan et al.
(2024) offer differing views. Because ToaST dra-
matically reduces high-frequency Leaf tokens, it
achieves substantially higher Rényi efficiency than
the baselines (Figure 8).20
for WordPiece and UnigramLM, respectively.
20Figure 16 in Section C gives a Zipf plot confirming that
ToaST has fewer high-frequency tokens in general. The Shan-
non efficiency with α = 1 is also better; see Figure 17 in
Section C.

Table 4: Downstream results (mean±std, n=4).
7
Downstream Results
To evaluate downstream performance, we trained
4 replications of a depth 24 nanochat language
model21 for ToaST and our baselines, varying the
random seed across training replicates.22 With a
vocabulary size of 40,960, each model had 1.56B
parameters. Pretraining used the default ClimbMix-
400B dataset (Diao et al., 2025). We replaced the
normal nanochat tokenizer training process with
the tokenizers we trained on CulturaX.
CORE, the primary downstream metric used by
nanochat, is an average over 22 benchmarks (Li
et al., 2025). As we see in Table 4, ToaST resulted
in the best CORE score and the best individual
score on 13 of the 22 tasks.23 On CORE, ToaST
significantly outperformed BPE by 7.6% (p=0.009)
and Unigram by 5.3% (p=0.032), and beat Word-
Piece by 2.6% (not significant).24 Since pretrain-
ing is token-matched on 5.9B tokens, ToaST’s su-
perior compression means the language models
trained with ToaST saw approximately 11% more
text than BPE, which may account for the strong
CORE performance. There is also a supervised fine
tuning (SFT) phase that is not compute matched,
giving ToaST fewer training steps due to its su-
perior compression. On the six SFT tasks in the
bottom of Table 4, Unigram—which has the worst
compression—was best on three, despite winning
only three of the 22 base tasks.
8
Conclusion
We presented ToaST, a tokenization method that
constructs split trees from byte n-gram counts and
selects a vocabulary by formulating an integer pro-
gram (IP) that minimizes the total token count un-
21https://github.com/karpathy/nanochat
22These models were trained on an AWS p4de.24xlarge
instance with 8xA100 80GB GPUs, taking approximately 9
hours each.
23See Table 6 in Section D for the individual CORE task
scores.
24Welch’s t-test, n=4.
der a recursive split tree inference procedure. The
LP relaxation of this IP is very tight, with a worst-
case optimality gap of less than one part per million
across all instances tested, making the approach
both principled and practical.
For vocabulary sizes of 40,960 and larger, ToaST
achieves over 11% better compression than BPE,
WordPiece, and UnigramLM, with dramatically
fewer single-byte tokens and higher Rényi effi-
ciency. These improvements stem from the global
optimization: unlike greedy merge-based methods,
ToaST selects tokens that collectively minimize the
token count, avoiding the intermediate tokens that
BPE retains as stepping stones to forming frequent
words. ToaST had the best downstream perfor-
mance on CORE by 2.6%–7.6% over baselines,
significantly outperforming BPE and Unigram, and
was best on 13 of 22 individual tasks.
Split tree construction is decoupled from vocab-
ulary selection: the trees are built once from corpus
statistics, and the IP finds an optimal vocabulary re-
gardless of how they were constructed. This separa-
tion means the splitting procedure can be extended
with a hierarchy of preferences without changing
the vocabulary optimization. For example, one
can prefer superwords, then morpheme-aligned
splits, then character boundary splits, then byte-
level splits. Similarly, the linear cost objective can
be reweighted to give equal influence to each lan-
guage on a multilingual dataset, directly addressing
tokenization premiums (Petrov et al., 2023; Arnett
et al., 2024) for low-resource languages. We sketch
these extensions in detail in Section A.
Limitations
Our experiments were conducted exclusively on
English text, and it is not clear how these results
will extend to other languages. Training on the
top aggregated pretokens may be less effective in
other languages, especially non-space-delimited
ones, as they would cover a lower fraction of the
8


--- Page 9 ---
total pretoken counts.
LLM Use Disclosure
We used Claude Code Opus 4.6 (Anthropic, 2026)
to help with the implementation of the code, before
manually verifying its correctness. The LLM was
also used to write the Python scripts to generate the
figures in this paper, and to improve wording at a
paragraph level.
Acknowledgments
This research was supported in part by the Israel
Science Foundation (grant No. 1166/23).

A
Extensions and Future Work
Since split tree construction is independent of vo-
cabulary selection, it is very flexible: any procedure
that produces binary trees can be used, potentially
with multiple sources of external data. While we
suggest a procedure based on byte n-gram counts
(choosing the split maximizing min(cp, cp′)), there
are natural extensions. Common pretoken combi-
nations could be combined into superwords (Liu
et al., 2025; Schmidt et al., 2025, 2026) that are
represented as a single split tree.
Morphemes
are the smallest linguistically meaningful units of
words, and ideally these units should be preserved
whenever possible (Ataman and Federico, 2018;
Bostrom and Durrett, 2020; Park et al., 2021; Hof-
mann et al., 2021, 2022; Limisiewicz et al., 2024).
Many scripts use multiple bytes to represent a char-
acter, and it is preferable to not split within the
multi-byte characters (Land and Arnett, 2025; Land
and Bartolo, 2024; Jang et al., 2025). Such a hierar-
chy of preferences can easily be incorporated into
the split tree construction.
Figure 9 gives an example of this hierarchy. The
phrase ␣crème␣brûlée could form a two-word su-
perword. Within the pretoken ␣brûlée, a mor-
phological analysis would suggest a split between
␣brûl and ée. The phrase’s three two-byte char-
acters should not be split in between the bytes. Fi-
nally, at the lowest level, we have the regular byte
splits we have previously discussed in split trees.
At each node, the split is chosen from the highest
available level in the preference hierarchy, using
the min(cp, cp′) criterion from Section 2 to select
among splits within a level. All results in this paper
11


--- Page 12 ---
0xc3 0xa8
0xc3 0xbb
0xc3
0xa9
c
r
m
e
b
r
l
e
0xc3 0xa8
0xc3 0xbb
0xc3
0xa9
c
r
m
e
b
r
l
e
0xc3 0xa8
0xc3 0xbb
0xc3
0xa9
c
r
m
e
b
r
l
e
0xc3 0xa8
0xc3 0xbb
0xc3
0xa9
c
r
m
e
b
r
l
e
è
û
é
c
r
m
e
b
r
l
e
0xc3 0xa8
0xc3 0xbb
0xc3
0xa9
c
r
m
e
b
r
l
e
byte splits
pretoken splits
morpheme splits
character splits
byte form
character form
Figure 9: Example split hierarchy of pretokens ≻morphemes ≻characters ≻bytes for crème brûlée.
use the multi-byte character level, as it requires
no additional external data, although its effect on
English is minimal since English text is almost en-
tirely single-byte characters. Pretoken n-grams for
superword construction and gold morpheme splits
could be added to support the other levels.
The vocabulary construction process can also
be customized, as the explicit linear objective in
Eq (5) allows different cj values to be used. As
one example, multilingual tokenizers often dispro-
portionately favor languages with the most training
data. This problem has been widely studied (Rust
et al., 2021; Ahia et al., 2023; Petrov et al., 2023;
Arnett et al., 2024; Ali et al., 2024; Teklehaymanot
and Nejdl, 2025; Dixit and Dixit, 2026; Lundin
et al., 2026) with a number of proposed solutions
(Foroutan et al., 2025; Churchill and Skiena, 2026).
Similarly to how imbalanced classification is of-
ten addressed by upweighting the cost coefficients
of minority classes, one could scale the cj so that
the total weight per language is equal, giving each
language equal influence in the optimization.
B
Additional Computational Results
B.1
Count Distribution of Pretokens
Table 5 gives the percent of cumulative total counts
of unique pretokens for various numbers of split
trees. While there are 58,469,075 unique pretokens
in the CulturaX training data, the 700k most fre-
quent pretokens cover more than 99% of the total
counts. At the smallest level of 400k split trees con-
sidered in Section 6, there was 98.64% coverage.
B.2
LP Model Size
Each split tree for pretoken j is a full binary tree
with |Lj| leaves (one per byte), giving exactly
|Kj| = 2|Lj| −1 nodes. The model has |I| vari-
ables xi (one per unique candidate token across
all trees) and P
j(2|Lj| −1) variables zjk (one
per node). The constraints consist of one vocabu-
lary size constraint, P
j(2|Lj|−1) link constraints
(Eq (9)), and P
j |Lj| leaf coverage constraints
(Eq (8)), for a total of 1+P
j(3|Lj|−1) constraints.
Since each unique token candidate corresponds to
at least one tree node, |I| ≤P
j(2|Lj| −1) + |B|,
both the number of variables and constraints are
linear in the total byte-length of the pretokens
P
j |Lj|. Typically |I| is much smaller than this
bound due to candidate tokens being shared across
many trees. As we see in the following section, the
length of our pretokens is bounded and small in
practice, so model size is also linear in the number
of pretokens used in training.
B.3
Pre-tokenization Regular Expression
We use the length-limited variant of the standard
GPT-4o regular expressions (regex) shown in List-
ing 2 to avoid very large trees resulting from long
runs of whitespace. To limit the length of the result-
ing match, we replace * with {0,32} for words or
{0,16} for other branches. We also replace + with
{1,32} for words or {1,16} for other branches. We
give the standard GPT-4o regex for comparison.25
B.4
Split tree inference speed
On a MacBook Pro, split tree inference in Python
achieves approximately 600k tokens/s compared to
1,000k tokens/s for the Rust-based Hugging Face
BPE implementation, only a factor of 1.6x slower.
This competitiveness is partly because inference
frequently terminates at the root node — when the
full pretoken is in the vocabulary, no recursion is
needed.
25From
https://github.com/openai/tiktoken/blob/4560a889/
tiktoken_ext/openai_public.py#L101-L114.
12

Table 5: Cumulative weighted count of unique preto-
ken types in CulturaX English training data, ranked by
frequency. The top 700k pretokens cover 99% of all
occurrences.
# length limited GPT-4o pattern
# limit punctuation and spaces to runs
# of no more than 16 characters
# and words to 32 characters (68 in the worse case)
# in practice it matches no more than 65 characters
GPT4O_REGEX_16 = "|".join([
r"[^\r\n\p{L}\p{N}]?[\p{Lu}\p{Lt}\p{Lm}\p{Lo}\p{M}]{0,32}\
[\p{Ll}\p{Lm}\p{Lo}\p{M}]{1,32}(?i:'s|'t|'re|'ve|'m|'ll|'d)?",
r"[^\r\n\p{L}\p{N}]?[\p{Lu}\p{Lt}\p{Lm}\p{Lo}\p{M}]{1,32}\
[\p{Ll}\p{Lm}\p{Lo}\p{M}]{0,32}(?i:'s|'t|'re|'ve|'m|'ll|'d)?",
r"\p{N}{1,3}",
r" ?[^\s\p{L}\p{N}]{1,16}[\r\n/]{0,16}",
r"\s{0,15}[\r\n]{1,16}",
r"\s{1,16}(?!\S)",
r"\s{1,16}",
])
# GPT-4o pattern for comparison
GPT4O_REGEX = "|".join([
r"[^\r\n\p{L}\p{N}]?[\p{Lu}\p{Lt}\p{Lm}\p{Lo}\p{M}]*\
[\p{Ll}\p{Lm}\p{Lo}\p{M}]+(?i:'s|'t|'re|'ve|'m|'ll|'d)?",
r"[^\r\n\p{L}\p{N}]?[\p{Lu}\p{Lt}\p{Lm}\p{Lo}\p{M}]+\
[\p{Ll}\p{Lm}\p{Lo}\p{M}]*(?i:'s|'t|'re|'ve|'m|'ll|'d)?",
r"\p{N}{1,3}",
r" ?[^\s\p{L}\p{N}]+[\r\n/]*",
r"\s*[\r\n]+",
r"\s+(?!\S)",
r"\s+",
])
Listing 2: We use a length limited variant of the standard
GPT-4o regular expressions (regex) to avoid very large
trees resulting from long runs of whitespace.
B.5
Resolving Multiple Vocabulary Sizes
The specified vocabulary size m appears in the
LP only through the right-hand side of Eq (6).
This makes it efficient to solve the model for a
range of vocabulary sizes. We first solve the LP
for the largest value, m = 262, 144, which takes
12–23 hours depending on the number of split trees.
We then decrease m and re-solve using a warm
start, reusing the previous optimal basis as the
starting point, which allows the solver to converge
much faster than solving from scratch. Under dual
simplex, the previous optimal basis remains dual
feasible since only the right-hand side of Eq (6)
changes, although it is no longer primal feasible
for the smaller value of m. Dual simplex can then
efficiently re-optimize from this basis, which is
substantially cheaper than solving each LP from
scratch.
Figure 10 gives the cumulative total time to set
up and solve the model, as a function of the number
of vocabulary sizes computed. Starting from the
largest vocabulary size m = 262, 144, we reduce
the size by 8,192 and resolve the LP using a warm
start. This continues down to the smallest vocabu-
lary size of 8,192. The time for subsequent resolves
is much reduced to less than an hour because of
the warm start. The 700k split tree problem took
notably longer at vocabulary sizes of 16,384 and
8,192, a pattern that was consistent across two in-
dependent runs. We do not have a clear explanation
for this behavior.
Figure 11 gives the individual timing of each
resolve step, with the same setup as Figure 10, with
a log scale on the y-axis solve time. In all runs,
the times decrease as the vocabulary size decreases
until it reaches a size of 40,960. The times then
increase again to the smallest sizes.

8
12
16

model, as a function of the number of vocabulary sizes
computed.

C
Additional Intrinsic Metrics
Figure 12 shows the spread in compression as the
number of split trees is varied, at the upper end
of vocabulary sizes where this spread is largest.
While the improvement in compression is small in
absolute terms, using more split trees is better in
all cases.

Figure 12: Validation bytes per token of ToaST, zoomed
in for larger vocabulary sizes.
Figure 13 shows the counts of the four categories
of tokens on validation data. While not visible in
the linear-scale graph in Figure 6, the counts of
Leaves and Non-Leaf Subword categories continue
to decrease as the vocabulary size increases, while
the number of Root tokens increases.
Figure 14 and Figure 15 show the four token
categories for WordPiece and UnigramLM, respec-
tively. Much like BPE in Figure 7, these have a
significant number of Leaf or single byte tokens
compared to ToaST (Figure 6).
Figure 16 gives a Zipf plot comparison of ToaST
with BPE, WordPiece, and UnigramLM. ToaST has
fewer very-frequent tokens compared to the base-
lines, and also has fewer very-infrequent tokens,

with a minimum count on the validation data of
103 for ToaST versus 4 for UnigramLM and 1 for
BPE and WordPiece.26
Figure 17 shows that the Shannon efficiency,
which corresponds to a Rényi efficiency with α =
1, is higher for ToaST than for the baselines.
Figure 18 shows a Venn diagram of the vocabu-
lary overlap on the validation data of ToaST, BPE
and UnigramLM at a vocabulary size of 128k.
While 42,363 tokens are shared by all three, Uni-
gramLM is the most distinctive, with 75,331 (57%)
of its tokens unique to it. In comparison, ToaST
has 20,556 unique tokens, while BPE has 15,272.
D
Downstream CORE Scores
CORE is the primary downstream metric used by
nanochat, which is an average over 22 benchmarks
(Li et al., 2025) shown in Table 6. CORE is evalu-
ated both on the base model and a supervised fine
26At a vocabulary size of 128k, ToaST has just 6 unused
tokens over the 37.5GB of validation data, which are all rare
single bytes added to avoid unknown tokens. The baseline
models have 24–92 unused tokens.

Table 8: LP relaxation gap statistics for the ToaST vocabulary selection MILP with 500k pretokens. The frac-x and
frac-z columns count fractional variables. The ∆= ˆf −f ∗
LP column is the difference between the objective of the
rounded LP solution ˆf and the optimal objective of the LP f ∗
LP. The δ = ∆/ ˆf column gives the relative relaxation
gap.

Table 10: LP relaxation gap statistics for the ToaST vocabulary selection MILP with 700k pretokens. The frac-x
and frac-z columns count fractional variables. The ∆= ˆf −f ∗
LP column is the difference between the objective
of the rounded LP solution ˆf and the optimal objective of the LP f ∗
